text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY CLINIC NOTE
Department of Medical Oncology

ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
CO-SIGNED: [Fellow/APP]

VISIT TYPE: Established Patient — Disease Status Assessment and Goals of Care

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:

Margaret Choi is a 60-year-old woman with metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma who presents today for oncology follow-up after her most recent restaging imaging, which showed clear disease progression on second-line nanoliposomal irinotecan (nal-IRI, Onivyde) plus 5-FU/leucovorin. Today's visit was scheduled specifically to review the imaging results in detail, discuss her current functional status and symptom burden, and engage in a thorough goals-of-care conversation with her and her family.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Choi is a 60-year-old librarian with a complex oncologic history spanning approximately 18 months. She was initially diagnosed with borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head/uncinate process (cT3 N1 M0, Stage III) after presenting with epigastric pain radiating to the back, new-onset obstructive jaundice, and 12 lbs of weight loss. EUS-guided fine needle biopsy confirmed moderately differentiated ductal adenocarcinoma. Comprehensive genomic profiling (FoundationOne CDx) revealed KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, SMAD4 R361H, ARID1A Q1178*, and STK11 L67fs*35; microsatellite stable (MSS), TMB-low, pMMR, no BRCA1/2 mutations, no actionable fusions. SMAD4 loss by IHC was noted as a poor prognostic feature.

She received neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX for 8 cycles with excellent biochemical (CA 19-9 decline from 1,248 to 85 U/mL) and radiographic response (tumor decreased from 3.8 cm to 2.4 cm, with resolution of SMA abutment and restoration of SMV fat plane). She then completed consolidative chemoradiation (50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent capecitabine, per institutional protocol). Restaging showed maintained resectability and she subsequently underwent a pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) — technically a challenging operation given the prior borderline vascular relationships, though ultimately an R0 resection was achieved with negative all margins including the uncinate/SMA margin. Final surgical pathology demonstrated ypT2 ypN2 M0 (CAP tumor regression grade 2, moderate response; 4/24 lymph nodes positive). The postoperative course was complicated by a Grade B pancreatic fistula and Grade B delayed gastric emptying, extending her hospital stay to 18 days; these resolved with conservative management and she was discharged home with a JP drain, TPN during the fistula recovery, and octreotide. She was subsequently started on pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (Creon) and insulin for new post-pancreatectomy diabetes, adding to her previously existing type 2 diabetes managed with metformin (now discontinued).

Given the pN2 disease, SMAD4 loss, and other high-risk pathologic features, she proceeded with adjuvant gemcitabine plus capecitabine (GemCap, per ESPAC-4 data). Her initial CA 19-9 post-surgery was 22 U/mL. Unfortunately, during adjuvant therapy (after cycle 3), two small indeterminate hepatic lesions were identified on restaging CT (6 mm and 5 mm in segments VI and VII), prompting an MRI liver with hepatobiliary contrast (Eovist). MRI demonstrated features highly concerning for hepatic metastases — rim enhancement with arterial phase, washout, and hypointensity on hepatobiliary phase. CT-guided core needle biopsy of the segment VI lesion confirmed metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma (CK7+, CK20-, CDX2-, SMAD4 loss). At that point her CA 19-9 had risen to 58 U/mL. This represented Stage IV (M1) disease developing during adjuvant GemCap, designating her disease as resistant to both FOLFIRINOX and GemCap.

Following goals-of-care discussion at multidisciplinary tumor board, she transitioned to second-line palliative therapy with the NAPOLI-1 regimen: nal-IRI 70 mg/m2 plus leucovorin plus 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 continuous infusion over 46 hours every 14 days. Importantly, prior to initiation, UGT1A1 genotyping was performed given the irinotecan-based backbone; she was found to be UGT1A1 *28/*28 (homozygous), conferring high risk of irinotecan toxicity, and her nal-IRI dose was prospectively reduced to 50 mg/m2 at the start. Despite this pre-emptive dose reduction, she experienced Grade 3 diarrhea during cycle 2 (requiring a 2-day hospitalization for IV rehydration and electrolyte repletion) and Grade 3 neutropenia (ANC nadir 0.6 × 10^9/L). Nal-IRI was further reduced to 35 mg/m2 and 5-FU was reduced by 25% thereafter. Her ECOG performance status worsened to 2-3 during cycle 3. She completed 4 cycles in total.

Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis after 4 cycles (results reviewed today in detail) showed clear disease progression: the segment VI hepatic lesion has grown from 0.9 cm to 2.1 cm, the segment VII lesion from 0.7 cm to 1.6 cm, and two new hepatic lesions have appeared (1.2 cm in segment IV; 0.8 cm in segment VIII). CA 19-9 has risen markedly to 312 U/mL. There is no evidence of peritoneal disease or pulmonary metastases on this scan, though a new finding of mild-to-moderate ascites was noted on clinical examination today (not explicitly reported on the CT but likely was present or early at time of imaging; see physical exam below).

Ms. Choi has now received three lines of therapy — mFOLFIRINOX (neoadjuvant), GemCap (adjuvant), and nal-IRI/5-FU/LV (second-line palliative). She has had meaningful exposure to all major active cytotoxic agents used in pancreatic cancer. Her tumor genomic profile offers no currently approved targeted therapies: her KRAS G12V is not covered by approved KRAS inhibitors in PDAC at this time; she is MSS/pMMR (no pembrolizumab indication); TMB-low (no tumor mutational burden-based immunotherapy indication); no BRCA1/2 pathogenic mutations (no olaparib/rucaparib indication); no NTRK fusions (no larotrectinib/entrectinib indication); no BRAF V600E. No clinical trials are available for which she would qualify given her current performance status.

She presents today accompanied by her husband and her adult daughter. She reports worsening abdominal bloating and fullness over the past 2 weeks, increased pain (now requiring more frequent use of breakthrough opioids), significant fatigue, and markedly reduced appetite. She is spending the majority of her waking hours in bed or in a recliner. She is able to perform limited self-care (washing her face, brushing teeth) but requires assistance for dressing and bathing. She is eating perhaps 20-30% of her usual intake. She has had no fevers. She has not had productive bowel movements and is passing only small amounts of stool — she attributes this to her reduced oral intake. She has experienced nausea, particularly after eating.

Pain: She is currently on oral sustained-release oxycodone 20 mg every 12 hours with oxycodone 5 mg immediate-release every 4 hours as needed for breakthrough pain. She reports using 3-4 breakthrough doses daily over the past week, suggesting her baseline opioid regimen is inadequate.

She and her family have been emotionally processing this trajectory over the past several weeks. Her husband reports she has been tearful at times but is clear-headed and engaged in conversations about her wishes. She has previously expressed that quality of life is paramount at this stage and does not wish to "be in the hospital or on machines." She has not yet formally completed a POLST or updated her advance directive but had expressed these preferences verbally.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

Constitutional: Significant fatigue. Anorexia. Weight loss (patient reports approximately 6-7 lbs over the past 3-4 weeks). Generalized weakness. No fevers or chills.
Gastrointestinal: Abdominal bloating and distension (new, worsening). Nausea, particularly postprandial. Decreased bowel movements. No hematemesis, no melena, no frank rectal bleeding. Epigastric and right upper quadrant discomfort, chronic but worsened.
Muscle/Neurologic: Residual bilateral hand and foot paresthesias from prior oxaliplatin exposure (stable/chronic, mild-to-moderate). Generalized weakness.
Respiratory: Mild dyspnea with exertion (climbing stairs), improved when recumbent. No cough.
Musculoskeletal: Back pain at mid-thoracic and lumbar levels, approximately 5/10 at rest, 7-8/10 with movement. This has worsened over the past 2 weeks.
Psychosocial: Anxiety about disease trajectory; actively engaged in family discussions. No active suicidal ideation. Tearfulness at times but not meeting criteria for clinical depression per patient report.
All other systems reviewed and negative or not contributing.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 102/64 mmHg, heart rate 98 bpm, respiratory rate 18/min, temperature 37.0°C, SpO2 96% on room air. Weight: 49.2 kg (BMI approximately 18.5 — significant weight loss from initial presentation). She has lost approximately 6 lbs since her last visit 6 weeks ago.

General: Ms. Choi is a thin, frail-appearing woman in mild distress related to fatigue and discomfort. She is alert, oriented, and engaged in conversation though visibly fatigued. She is seated in a wheelchair brought by her family. She appears older than her stated age of 60.

Head/Eyes/ENT: Mild scleral icterus is noted bilaterally. Mucous membranes are dry. No oral thrush. No temporal wasting though buccal fat pads are diminished.

Neck: No palpable lymphadenopathy. No JVD.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses diminished in bilateral lower extremities, 1+ distally, likely consistent with nutritional/volume status.

Respiratory: Breath sounds are reduced at bilateral lung bases with dullness to percussion. Small bilateral pleural effusions cannot be excluded. No wheezes. Breathing is somewhat labored with exertion.

Abdomen: Protuberant and distended. Moderate ascites is present — shifting dullness is present and fluid wave is elicited. Diffuse tenderness to palpation, most pronounced in the right upper quadrant and epigastrium. Hepatomegaly palpable approximately 4 cm below the right costal margin, firm and slightly nodular. Splenic tip not palpable. Bowel sounds present but hypoactive. Surgical scar from prior Whipple procedure well-healed.

Extremities: Bilateral lower extremity pitting edema, 2+ at ankles and extending to mid-calf. This is new compared with her last visit. Bilateral stocking-glove distribution sensory changes consistent with known oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x 3. Cranial nerves grossly intact. No asterixis assessed. Moderate bilateral hand and foot paresthesias on subjective report.

Skin: Mild scleral and cutaneous icterus. Skin turgor mildly reduced. No rashes.

Functional assessment: ECOG Performance Status 3 — capable of only limited self-care, confined to bed or chair more than 50% of waking hours.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today or within 72 hours):

Complete Blood Count:
  - WBC: 6.8 × 10^9/L
  - Hemoglobin: 9.4 g/dL (normocytic anemia, likely anemia of chronic disease/malignancy)
  - Platelets: 112 × 10^9/L
  - ANC: 3.9 × 10^9/L

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium: 131 mEq/L (hyponatremia, likely dilutional in setting of ascites)
  - Potassium: 3.3 mEq/L (borderline hypokalemia)
  - Bicarbonate: 20 mEq/L
  - BUN: 22 mg/dL
  - Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL
  - Glucose: 214 mg/dL (elevated — insulin regimen likely subtherapeutic in current malnourished state; patient not eating consistently)
  - Total Bilirubin: 2.1 mg/dL (mildly elevated — consistent with hepatic parenchymal replacement and/or early cholestasis from tumor burden)
  - Direct Bilirubin: 1.5 mg/dL
  - AST: 78 U/L (elevated)
  - ALT: 52 U/L (mildly elevated)
  - Alkaline Phosphatase: 312 U/L (significantly elevated — consistent with hepatic metastases)
  - Albumin: 2.4 g/dL (severely low — significant hypoalbuminemia reflecting malnutrition, hepatic dysfunction, and systemic inflammation)
  - Total Protein: 5.8 g/dL
  - LDH: 488 U/L (elevated)

Coagulation:
  - PT: 14.8 sec; INR: 1.4 (mild coagulopathy consistent with hepatic dysfunction)
  - PTT: 34 sec

Tumor Markers:
  - CA 19-9: 312 U/mL (markedly elevated; up from 58 U/mL at initiation of second-line therapy)
  - CEA: 14.7 ng/mL (elevated)

Other:
  - C-reactive protein: 88 mg/L (elevated, systemic inflammatory state)
  - Lipase: 94 U/L (mildly elevated, within context of prior pancreatic surgery)

---

IMAGING RESULTS:

Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (obtained approximately 10 days ago, after completion of 4 cycles of nal-IRI/5-FU/LV) was reviewed in detail today:
- Hepatic metastases have grown substantially and new lesions have appeared: segment VI lesion now 2.1 cm (was 0.9 cm), segment VII lesion now 1.6 cm (was 0.7 cm), new lesion segment IV 1.2 cm, new lesion segment VIII 0.8 cm. Collectively the liver is heavily involved and the parenchyma appears diffusely heterogeneous.
- No locoregional recurrence in the surgical bed.
- No peritoneal implants or omental caking identified, though sensitivity of CT for peritoneal disease is limited.
- No pulmonary nodules or masses.
- Mild perihepatic and perisplenic fluid is present on this scan, consistent with early ascites that has now become clinically apparent on examination.
- The metal biliary stent from the pre-Whipple ERCP is no longer in situ (resected with specimen).

Prior imaging reviewed: The trajectory of radiographic progression is clear across the entire treatment course.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Choi is a 60-year-old woman with metastatic (Stage IV, M1 liver) pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, now with unequivocal disease progression on third-line (second-line palliative) nanoliposomal irinotecan/5-FU/leucovorin. She has an ECOG performance status of 3, worsening hepatic function, new moderate ascites, bilateral lower extremity edema, and a CA 19-9 of 312 U/mL. She has received all major active cytotoxic chemotherapy regimens available for her disease (oxaliplatin-based via FOLFIRINOX, gemcitabine/capecitabine, and irinotecan-based via nal-IRI/5-FU/LV). Her tumor genomic profile, while complex, harbors no currently approved targeted vulnerabilities (no BRCA, MSS/pMMR, no TMB-high, no NTRK fusion, KRAS G12V without applicable approved inhibitor in PDAC). She does not have KRAS G12C or any other currently actionable KRAS variant.

I had an extensive, compassionate, and unhurried discussion today with Ms. Choi and her family (husband and daughter present for the entire visit). We reviewed:

1. The imaging findings in plain language — that the cancer has continued to grow in the liver and new tumors have appeared despite the most recent chemotherapy.
2. The status of her disease: she has now progressed through all standard and most reasonable treatment options for her cancer type.
3. The option of further chemotherapy (e.g., FOLFOX, single-agent nab-paclitaxel): given her current performance status of 3, worsening liver function with hypoalbuminemia and mild hyperbilirubinemia, and significant ascites, the risks of further cytotoxic therapy substantially and clearly outweigh any potential benefit. Patients with PS 3-4 are generally excluded from chemotherapy trials for good reason — the risk of fatal toxicity is high and meaningful benefit is unlikely. I shared this clearly with the patient and family.
4. The option of clinical trial enrollment: I have reviewed all available trials at our institution and at nearby centers. She does not meet eligibility criteria for active trials based on PS 3 and current hepatic function parameters.
5. The option of best supportive/comfort-focused care with hospice enrollment: I explained what hospice entails — that it is a comprehensive support system focused entirely on her quality of life, comfort, and dignity at home, with around-the-clock nursing support, medication management for pain and symptoms, social work, chaplaincy, and bereavement support for the family.

Ms. Choi and her family were tearful but engaged throughout this discussion. She stated clearly: "I don't want any more chemo. I want to be home. I want to feel comfortable." Her husband and daughter are in agreement and fully supportive. This is consistent with prior expressed wishes.

PRIMARY PLAN: Transition to comfort-focused care / hospice.

**Hospice Referral:**
- Inpatient hospice intake consultation requested for today to initiate the formal evaluation and paperwork. The social worker is being paged to join the care team today.
- Patient and family agree to home hospice as the preferred setting.
- Transitioning from all active chemotherapy as of today — nal-IRI/5-FU/LV is discontinued.

**Pain Management:**
- Current regimen (oxycodone SR 20 mg q12h, oxycodone IR 5 mg q4h PRN) is clearly insufficient given 3-4 breakthrough doses per day.
- Pain plan revised: Increase sustained-release oxycodone to 30 mg every 12 hours. Increase immediate-release oxycodone to 10 mg every 3 hours as needed for breakthrough pain (approximately 10-15% of total daily dose).
- Bowel regimen prescribed: senna-docusate combination given opioid use and reduced mobility. Methylnaltrexone (Relistor) discussed as an option if opioid-induced constipation becomes refractory.
- Patient and husband counseled on how to use the breakthrough dosing appropriately. Written instructions provided.
- Hospice team will manage all opioid titration going forward.

**Ascites:**
- Symptomatic ascites present. Patient reports abdominal fullness and discomfort.
- Paracentesis discussed for symptomatic relief. Patient prefers to defer for now given discomfort of the procedure — will be reassessed by the hospice team and patient may choose this if symptoms worsen.
- Patient instructed to limit sodium to reduce fluid retention. However, given goals are comfort-focused, dietary restrictions should not limit enjoyment of food — a light sodium approach only.
- No diuretics started — given hyponatremia (Na 131) and low albumin, diuretic use carries significant risk of renal impairment.

**Diabetes Management:**
- Basal-bolus insulin regimen simplification: given reduced oral intake and comfort-focused goals, aggressive glucose management is no longer appropriate.
- Recommend transitioning to a simplified regimen or holding insulin corrections. The hospice team's nursing staff will monitor glucose primarily for symptoms of hypoglycemia. No fingerstick monitoring is required unless symptomatic.
- Metformin (previously discontinued post-Whipple) will not be restarted.

**Pancreatic Enzyme Replacement:**
- Creon can be continued if she is eating for comfort, adjusted as intake permits, but not a medical priority given goals of care.

**Nausea:**
- Ondansetron 4 mg orally or sublingual every 8 hours as needed continued.
- Haloperidol 0.5-1 mg sublingual every 6 hours as needed for refractory nausea discussed as a useful adjunct under hospice management.
- Dexamethasone 2-4 mg daily discussed for appetite stimulation and nausea in the setting of hepatic malignancy — this can improve quality of life meaningfully in the short term. Prescription provided; the hospice physician will titrate.

**Edema:**
- Bilateral lower extremity edema noted. Given comfort goals and the hyponatremia/low albumin, diuretics are not recommended. Gentle elevation of legs when in bed or chair. Supportive compression stockings can be used if tolerated.

**Nutrition:**
- Dietitian involvement via hospice program recommended.
- No artificial nutrition (NG tube, TPN) indicated at this stage — not consistent with goals of care and has not been shown to benefit patients in this clinical setting.
- Encourage small, frequent meals of whatever she enjoys. No dietary restrictions except patient comfort.

**Emotional and Psychosocial Support:**
- Palliative care and social work are engaged.
- Chaplaincy services offered — patient accepted.
- Bereavement support resources discussed with the family.
- We discussed advance directive completion: strongly encouraged patient and husband to formalize her POLST (Physician Orders for Life-Sustaining Treatment) and update her advance directive with the hospice team. Social worker will assist with this today.

**Follow-up:**
- No further oncology follow-up visits are planned at this institution outside of the transition to hospice — care will be fully managed by the hospice team.
- I provided my direct contact information to the family and encouraged them to call if they have questions or needs during the hospice transition.
- Hospice team will make first visit within 24-48 hours.

This has been an exceptionally challenging case and journey for Ms. Choi and her family. She received aggressive, multimodal treatment and achieved a meaningful period of disease control including successful surgical resection with negative margins — outcomes that speak to the effort she and her care team put forward. Despite these efforts, her cancer has ultimately progressed through multiple lines of therapy, consistent with the biology of SMAD4-null, KRAS-mutant, MSS pancreatic adenocarcinoma. We honor her wishes, support her comfort, and will continue to be available to her and her family through this transition.

Time spent: Approximately 75 minutes, of which greater than 50% was dedicated to counseling and care coordination.

[Electronically signed by Attending Oncologist]
[Cosigned by Fellow/APP]
